channel.title: Help: Research and Resources (new posts)
channel.description: Posts in the forum category "Help: Research and Resources" - Doing background research to use real-life knowledge to make in-universe articles sound professional.

item.title: Weapons a MTF might use: Re: Weapons a MTF might use
item.content:encoded: When dealing with some hardier anomalies, Mtf's may use anti-vehicle weapons instead of anti-personel. Like an rpg-7 for cost-effectiveness or a m203 grenade launcher for a underbarrel option for their m4 carbine or m16. Also a masterkey could be effective for breaking and entering during combat operations. Perhaps task forces may have access to weapons and tools of an anomalous nature.

item.title: What temperature can pizza be safely eaten?: Re: What temperature can pizza be safely eaten?
item.content:encoded:
If this is a question of what temperature food can be served at without being so hot as to burn a person's mouth, I found this thread which has one or two replies on it with info you may find helpful.

Someone experimenting with soup seemed to find 150F - 180F (65C - 82C) to be about the range of "still warm" to "piping hot".

item.title: What temperature can pizza be safely eaten?: Re: What temperature can pizza be safely eaten?
item.content:encoded: Theoretically speaking, you could just eat a raw pizza and nothing bad will happen to you, assuming the dough is cooked. That's really the only thing that needs to be cooked to be safe to eat, and it bakes at about 246°C. (For 15 minutes)

item.title: What temperature can pizza be safely eaten?: What temperature can pizza be safely eaten?
item.content:encoded:
I'm making a scp article involving a pizza. This pizza can maintains its heat but I'm trying figure out what temperature to adjust it to so people can safely consume it without burning their tongue. I tried looking it up but all it said is how the cook pizza in the oven.

So in your opinion, what temperature does a pizza have to be safely eaten without being burned and being tasty?

item.title: Real Life Publishing Opportunities: Re: Real Life Publishing Opportunities
item.content:encoded:
The Horror Tree provides a variety of news for authors of horror, including new calls for submissions, interviews, and more. Their about page reads:

About The Horror Tree

The Horror Tree is a resource for genre and speculative fiction authors which was created in 2011. The main goal when starting the site was to include all of the latest horror anthologies and publishers that are taking paying submissions. A resource useful for both new and experienced publishers alike looking for an outlet for their written material! Each anthology that we list should have a set deadline as well as offer compensation in the form of direct payment, royalties, or at the very least a physical contributor’s copy.

Since that time we have expanded to include articles on every aspect of the writing process, fiction, guest posts, blog tours, and more!

Note: We do not make any money from you submitting to these anthologies, we are not releasing them, nor are we responsible for them in any way. Our goal is just to try and help out our fellow horror authors to have an easy way to find out what anthologies are open to submit to and have a community to work with.

If you are a horror author, I hope this is helpful for you!
Stuart Conover
Editor in Chief

item.title: Aboriginal Australian Culture and Dreamtime: Re: Aboriginal Australian Culture and Dreamtime
item.content:encoded:
Disclaimer: I am not an aboriginal Australian. I do not claim to be an expert on their culture/practices

After reading a bit into the cultural impact of Koalas I found a connection to dreamtime on the Wikipedia page for Koalas, quote:

The Tharawal people believed that the animal helped row the boat that brought them to the continent.[140] Another myth tells of how a tribe killed a koala and used its long intestines to create a bridge for people from other parts of the world. This narrative highlights the koala's status as a game animal and the length of its intestines.

So at least two koala myths connect to travel between continents, which is an interesting connection. In terms of ceremonies that occur, I would recommend reading this article, as it lists a lot of ceremonies. Hope this helps!

item.title: Crime Scene Investigation Procedure: Re: Crime Scene Investigation Procedure
item.content:encoded:
Hi War!

This is a granular guide for law enforcement at crime scenes:

https://www.ncjrs.gov/pdffiles1/nij/178280.pdf

It may be worth bearing in mind that these are guidelines, and as we know American law enforcement can be wont to bend rules. That being said it might be of use.

item.title: Aboriginal Australian Culture and Dreamtime: Aboriginal Australian Culture and Dreamtime
item.content:encoded:
Hello everyone! I am currently writing an SCP concerning the disappearance of koalas due in part to the Dreamtime Serpent, and I am particularly looking for info concerning koala and Dreamtime lore, as well as personal experiences with regards to these cultures.

I would also like to get info on what ceremonies, lifestyle measures, and traditions are primarily practiced nowadays for environmental purposes. If possible, a personal experience with them, particularly in events such as bunya gatherings, will be valuable :)

Thank you to those who will provide information on these matters!

item.title: Crime Scene Investigation Procedure: Crime Scene Investigation Procedure
item.content:encoded:
Hey howdy hey, so I'm writing a tale at the moment that predominantly revolves around a crime scene. Thing is, I don't actually know very much about how actual crime scene procedures work, as the most popular point of reference tends to be dramatized TV shows.

I realise that in order to keep stuff interesting I'll need to strike a decent balance between real-world procedure and something a little more fictitious, but I'm not entirely sure where to look for real-world references. I've done some research, but I end up with a mixed bag of results that refer to how various countries handle it.

I'm looking for details of crime scene procedure as it would be in the real world particularly for the US - what kinda staff will be present, who's allowed where, how much can be done on-site that would contribute to the investigation, etc.
The crime takes place indoors (an apartment), and involves a dead body, if those details matter at all.

Articles for reference would be good, and if there's any online short story type deals that exist out there (I've had no luck finding any that aren't books) that would be fantastic.

item.title: World War Two Correspondence: Re: World War Two Correspondence
item.content:encoded:
Just from googling:
https://www.loc.gov/vets/stories/ex-war-correspondence.html

https://digital.shsmo.org/digital/collection/wwii/search/searchterm/C0068

https://www.bbc.co.uk/history/ww2peopleswar/categories/c09/ (probably British)

item.title: World War Two Correspondence: World War Two Correspondence
item.content:encoded: Hi all! Wondering if anyone had a good source to find letters home from WWII. Doing a bit of resource to get a more historically accurate tone and style of writing for a tale I'm doing. (Fingers crossed) Stuff from the Korean War would do fine as well. It's not a top priority request, but if anyone has any experience in the subject any pointers would be great.

item.title: Psychological Theories in 1800s: Re: Psychological Theories in 1800s
item.content:encoded: So uh… This may or may not be useful, but as I'm sure you know, in the nineteenth century there were all kinds of crazy "cures" for mental illness. One that really stands out as particularly crazy was called the Cox's Chair. It was basically a chair you would strap someone in, then spin the chair around really, really fast. (Like spinning in an office chair, except more intense.) You would then stop the chair suddenly, in the hope that the illness would be forcefully jerked out of the patient. (It didn't work) So if what you were looking for was an entity that tries to help people, but sort of… screws up, Cox's Chair could be an interesting addition. I hope you find what you're looking for, and good luck on your scip!

item.title: Psychological Theories in 1800s: Re: Psychological Theories in 1800s
item.content:encoded:
Sent you a PM.

In short, psychotherapy for mental illness (and even modern conceptions of mental illness as something with (partially) psychological and relationship-based causes rather than just a moral, spiritual, or physical cause) did not exist until the 1880's. Some of the people who Freud worked under in the 1880's were experimenting with hypnosis for "hysteria", but Freud was the first person to have a systematic theory and practice of therapy.

Before that in the 1800's, even the idea that mental illness had a physical/neurological cause, or the idea that people with mental illness might get better if you talk to them/don't treat them like complete shit/don't chain them up, were new ideas.

In terms of theories, most people were still basing theories on philosophy, most were interested in "normal" functioning people rather than mental illness, and the field of medicine itself was way less developed than it is now. Early psychologists were also more interested in studying "normal" perception and consciousness than abnormal psych (but, again, didn't exist as a separate field of study until the 1870s).

"Animal Magnetism" was a theory in the 1700's/early 1800's that led into hypnosis, but that was a theory of general human health/functioning and wasn't applied to mental illness until the 1880's. You could look at the Humors and Phrenology for ideas before that, but again, these were physical theories (with physical treatments in the case of humors) not "therapy."

Here are some links:
https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sigmund_Freud
https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Theodor_Meynert
https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jean-Martin_Charcot
https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philippe_Pinel

https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_psychiatry (read the early modern part)

Edit: Also, the British aristocracy really cratered because of WW1, so you're free to set your SCP in the 1890-1910 range. If you do that, here are a few other things to look at:
https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lightner_Witmer
https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emil_Kraepelin

New edit: This is also a good article: https://www.utoledo.edu/library/canaday/exhibits/quackery/quack5.html
It just covers asylums and medicine, though, it doesn't get inch Freud and psychotherapy or scientific psychology.

item.title: Real Life Publishing Opportunities: Re: Real Life Publishing Opportunities
item.content:encoded:
It means that your manuscript will be submitted without your name or identifying information. Your cover letter and whatever form you fill out when submitting the piece will contain your name, just not your manuscript itself when you submit it. This goal is such that if you are an incredibly famous author (or if you have a controversial name, etc) you cannot bias the editor in any way when they're selecting manuscripts. Certain editors believe that anonymizing manuscripts allows them to more objectively select pieces of work.

While I understand this may seem suspicious, please be rest assured that Diabolical Plots is a well regarded, SFWA qualifying market, and that if they do something untoward with your piece the reputation hit would be immense if other writers come to hear about it.

item.title: Real Life Publishing Opportunities: Re: Real Life Publishing Opportunities
item.content:encoded:
I'm interested in this, but I have no clue what this whole section means, and there's nothing to really clarify it:

We have followed a very strict anonymity policy in the past that has meant that stories that were not fully anonymized were immediately disqualified. After some discussion with people in publishing, we have decided to keep the preference for anonymity, but not have such dire consequences if it’s broken. Please try to keep your name out of the story and the story notes boxes. But if you forget, your story will not be disqualified for it.

Does that mean we can't put our names on our stories? Like, not even on the manuscript format? That… seems kinda sketchy.

item.title: Real Life Publishing Opportunities: Re: Real Life Publishing Opportunities
item.content:encoded:
OUT THERE: Into the Queer New Yonder is looking for YA queer futuristic science fiction.

The story requirements are simple:

4-8 thousand words
YA fiction set in the future (any genre)
Queer author, queer protagonist
The story you wish you had when you were 16
Does not have to be #ownvoices
Does not have to contain romantic elements

What you get if selected:

Publication in OUT THERE: Into the Queer New Yonder anthology, edited by Saundra Mitchell; published by Inkyard Press.
$900 (US) plus a pro-rated share of any royalties received on the sale of the anthology.
We ask for exclusive rights from acceptance to 2 years from publication date. Exceptions will be made for Best Of/Year Of/Awards anthologies.
All rights revert 2 years from publication date of OUT THERE anthology. (On resale, we’d appreciate a credit, “First appeared in OUT THERE, 2022.”)
We ask for publication and audio rights for that term. All other rights remain with you.
You must be at least 13 years old to submit to this anthology. Authors 13-18 years old must co-sign their contract with their parent/guardian if selected for publication.

item.title: I'm writing a SCP about a reality bending fish: Re: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: No problem, again, just remember to check next time.

item.title: I'm writing a SCP about a reality bending fish: Re: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: thanks im sorry i just get a lot of anxiety when something like this happens. Thanks for fixing it for me, i wont do it again i promise

item.title: I'm writing a SCP about a reality bending fish: Re: I'm writing a SCP about a reality bending fish
item.content:encoded:
Im thinking an alaskan trout

Look up examples of that species being kept in captivity. For example:
http://www.adfg.alaska.gov/index.cfm?adfg=wildlifenews.view_article&articles_id=891

item.title: I'm writing a SCP about a reality bending fish: Re: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded:
I didnt know what to do if it was a repeat thread or my original page would be lost

For future reference, you can look at the "recent posts and comments" section of your wikidot profile: http://www.wikidot.com/user:info/aleph-null99 to see if your post went through or not. If it did get posted, it would appear on your profile. Usually it's good to also wait a minute or two to be certain it's not just in the middle of loading.

am i getting banned for this

No, since it's just the one instance and it was by accident. A ban would result if you intentionally spammed multiple times after staff told you not to.
